topic: Traits - From, Into, and the Orphan Rule

turns.content: I've been thinking about how my hobby project handles data conversion, sort of like when I used to manually write a function in C to turn an int into a float. It feels repetitive though, so I'm wondering if Rust has a way to make this automatic without writing boilerplate code everywhere.

That sounds very similar to how the From and Into traits work in Rust. Think of it like a universal adapter: instead of writing your own conversion function for every single type pair, you just implement From<Source> on the target type, and then Into is automatically available because it's just a thin wrapper that delegates to From. This saves you from having to write repetitive boilerplate code everywhere.

That makes sense conceptually, but I'm running into an issue with my third-party library types. My custom data structure needs to be converted into the config format from that library, but Rust is telling me it can't implement From for the library's type because of some restriction.

You are encountering the orphan rule, which prevents you from implementing From<ThirdParty> for ThirdParty when neither your custom type nor ThirdParty implements traits like Display or Clone. The reason for this restriction is that Rust wants to ensure conversions remain safe and predictable; if one of the types already had methods defined by an external crate, allowing arbitrary implementations could break the abstraction boundaries those crates established.

So it's basically a safety net against accidentally leaking internal details or breaking contracts between libraries? And since I can't just implement From for the library type, what's my actual solution?

Exactly, it acts as a guard to prevent accidental dependency violations. Your solution is to use a newtype wrapper: you create your own simple struct that wraps the third-party type and then implement From for that wrapper. This gives you full control over the conversion logic without violating the orphan rule because only your custom type (the wrapper) implements From, not the external library's types.

I see, so I wrap the external data in my own struct to bridge the gap. But what if both of my custom types already implement their own traits? Does that change anything about how I should approach this conversion problem?

Not really; the orphan rule still applies based on whether either type has an existing trait implementation, so the newtype pattern remains your primary strategy. However, once you've set up the wrappers correctly and implemented From for them, you can then implement Into as a simple trait or just rely on the automatic delegation to From, allowing you to chain conversions cleanly in your code without worrying about manual function implementations.
